what: Overview of GLM levels used in fMRI analysis
title: fmri.pipeline overview and design
text: data science terms, task fMRI data fundamentally multilevel ‘clustered.’ , timepoints specific given run task runs nested within subject. Whereas many multilevel structures analyzed using multilevel regression models, challenging fMRI data couple reasons. First, run-level data time series data complex temporal characteristics (e.g., slow autocorrelation) must addressed strategies like prewhitening inclusion autocorrelation moving average parameters mitigate risk spurious anticonservative statistical tests result simple ordinary least squares model. Second, challenge carrying forward parameter estimates run-level models group analysis. simple fixed-effects approach include parameter estimates (aka ‘betas’) – one per subject. one subject precise betas (small standard errors) another noisy betas (large standard errors)? approach carrying forward betas alone incorporate uncertainties, noisy precise betas treated . Given challenges, fMRI software adopts approach separating estimation activation statistics across run, subject, sample levels. Ideally, approach propagate uncertainty estimates (usually, standard errors) one level model next. analogous meta-analyses pooled estimates effect (e.g., mean effect size cognitive-behavioral therapy) weighted (inversely) standard error individual study. present (July 2021), fmri.pipeline designed general structure support GLM analyses using software, including AFNI, FSL, SPM, R. However, machinery built FSL FEAT, think best features terms parameter estimation (e.g., Tukey tapering prewhitening Bayesian incorporation lower-level standard errors group analyses). FSL adopts approach analyzing run fMRI data separate GLM model. called ‘first-level’ analysis (aka Level 1) , fundamentally, time series analysis design regressors convolved hemodynamic response function (HRF) associations regressors BOLD estimated voxel. subject multiple runs task, integrated combining parameter estimates using fixed-effects weighted (standard errors) estimation approach. yields voxelwise estimate level 1 regressor (e.g., occurrence stimulus type) can carried forward group analysis. combination runs single subject called ‘second-level’ (aka Level 2) analysis. addition simply averaging first-level regressors, level 2 analyses can incorporate -run regressors capture categorical continuous predictors run--run differences. example, one expects decreases task-related activation additional run (task familiarity, habituation), linear effect run can included regressor level 2 model. design factor varies one run next (e.g., run 1 cat pictures run 2 dog pictures), can incorporated dummy codes level 2 capture -condition differences. simplest common model Level 2 simple intercept-model intercept included GLM design matrix. model yields weighted combination run-level estimates contrast estimated level 1. -run predictors interest, intercept-model sufficient prepare data ‘third-level’ (aka Level 3) analysis, estimates effects overall sample. comparison, software packages (e.g., AFNI), concatenate runs together include run-specific baseline regressors control intensity drift differences one run next. concatenated approach, two levels: subject-level analysis sample-level analysis. Throughout fmri.pipeline, adopt nomenclature three-level perspective fMRI data. sum : Level 1 run-level analysis BOLD time series. design matrix typically includes convolved regressors represent task-related events. Parameters estimated run separately. Level 2 subject-level analysis integrates Level 1 effects across runs. Level 2 design matrices can also include regressors capture -run manipulations covariates. Level 3 sample-level analysis estimates effects across subjects. design matrix includes -subjects predictors (e.g., Age trait anxiety) task-related activation.

Thus, 100 subjects 5 runs analyzed using 4 different level 1 models, 10054 = 2000 jobs need completed. comparison, number level 3 analyses much smaller since jobs group model interest. two ways parallelize computation: 1. use multiple cores within single compute job, 2. distribute computations set independent jobs scheduler. mutually exclusive approaches. example, 1000 models need estimate Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, number samples can increased single model multiple chains model, chain can parked separate core single computer. , use 4 cores per model increase samples, 1000 models run, likely submit 1000 jobs HPC scheduler (using sbatch SLURM qsub TORQUE), job request 4 compute cores. additional details considerations : https://psu-psychology.github.io/r-bootcamp-2018/talks/parallel_r.html. order accommodate different parallelization needs handle differences compute times wait times across high-performance cluster environments, $parallel_settings field $gpa object allows user specify much time many cores needed step pipeline. defaults (noted ), encouraged adjust values find models taking much longer shorter run.

title: Familywise error correction for voxelwise GLMs in fmri.pipeline
text: outlined Cox et al. (2017, Brain Connectivity), conventional Gaussian random field theory approach cluster correction fMRI analysis anticonservative many cases empirical autocorrelation function noise fMRI much longer tail Gaussian distribution predict. highlighted Woo & Wager (2014) elaborated Eklund (2016, greater effect). couple improvements proposed tested Cox control familywise error rate (nearly) .05. package supports methods. Let’s quick walkthrough.

title: Familywise error correction for voxelwise GLMs in fmri.pipeline
text: basic approach 3dClustSim (using -fwhm -fwhmxyz) assumes Gaussian-distributed noise structure. noted , realistic fMRI data. first correction attempts overcome assumption Gaussianity fitting long-tailed distribution noise structure empirical data. Specifically, one passes first-level GLM residuals (one volume per timepoint) inputs 3dFWHMx includes -ACF flag. flag estimate autocorrelation function data fitting parameters mono-exponential + Gaussian mixture can better accommodate long-tailed spatial autocorrelations fMRI data. 3dFWHMx produces ACF parameters every volume run’s residuals file. usual approach (per AFNI) take average parameters per run best estimate spatial structure noise run. , estimate cluster correction thresholds 3dClustSim, one averages run-averaged ACF parameters across runs achieve overall ACF entire dataset. Cox et al., 2017, median ACF parameters across datasets input 3dClustSim – suppose mean work, , depending whether outliers sway . estimated values averaged 3-parameter ACF passed 3dClustSim -acf argument. Based -acf input, 3dClustSim now estimates null datasets spatial distribution noise derived empirical data, better controls FWE relative standard Gaussian assumption/distribution. text: afni_3dfwhmx class wrapper around 3dFWHMx. typical ACF approach, pass average ACF parameters across datasets 3dClustSim -acf. , must provide one set ACF parameter estimates 3dClustSim -acf program can generate null datasets spatial autocorrelation structure. usually use 3dClustSim calculate FWE correction group analysis may reflect hundreds first-level datasets (fMRI runs). residuals first-level GLM must passed 3dFWHMx ACF parameters can inform overall (group) ACF estimates. Said differently, typical 3dClustSim -acf approach requires ACF parameter averaging two stages. First, take average ACF parameters across volumes first-level residuals. controlled -geom -arith flag 3dFWHMx, specifies volume-wise ACF estimates combined yield run-wise ACF. , average run-wise ACF parameters derive sample-wise ACF average reflects average spatial autocorrelation across datasets analysis. aid estimation ACF parameters across many runs, provide afni_3dfwhmx_list class. Conceptually, list afni_3dfwhmx objects, pertains single run data. goal afni_3fwhmx_list class run 3dFWHMx input datasets provide average values ACF across datasets. average can passed directly 3dClustSim -acf. ’s quick example:
code: # get ACF parameters for 8 first-level residual files for testing # this example is simpler than typical applications where we have many subject and many runs path <- "/proj/mnhallqlab/studies/MMClock/MR_Proc/10637_20140304/mni_5mm_aroma/sceptic_vchosen_ventropy_dauc_pemax_vtime_preconvolve" res4d_files <- list.files(pattern = "res4d.nii.gz", path = path, full.names = T, recursive = T) fwhmx_mask_files <- list.files(pattern = "mask.nii.gz", path = path, full.names = T, recursive = T)  # these are the files to be passed through 3dFWHMx print(res4d_files)  # create the list object requires you to provide input and mask files for each residuals dataset of interest. test <- afni_3dfwhmx_list$new(input_files = res4d_files, mask_files = fwhmx_mask_files, scheduler = "slurm")  # submit 3dFWHMx jobs to cluster -- the object will divide the list into smaller jobs if needed # if 3dFWHMx has completed for all inputs already, the $submit method will just return immediately and note # that there is nothing to submit. If you want to force re-estimation, use $submit(force = TRUE). test$submit()  # has 3dFWHMx completed for all datasets provided to the list? test$is_complete()  # average a, b, c parameters across datasets -- good for input to 3dClustSim -acf test$get_acf_average()  # average effective FWHM estimate using -ACF test$get_effective_fwhm()  # per-dataset ACF summary if you want to see what got averaged test$get_acf_df()

code: 1. Compute the residuals of the model at each voxel at the group level. 2. Generate a null distribution by randomizing among subjects the signs of the residuals in the test (and permuting subject data sets between groups, if doing a two-sample test without covariates),    repeat the t-tests (with covariates, if present), and iterate 10,000 times. 3. Take the 10,000 3D t-statistic maps from the randomization and use those as input to 3dClustSim (with no additional smoothing or random deviate generation):    threshold the maps at a large number of p values (e.g., 0.01, 0.005, and 0.001), clusterize them, then record the maximum cluster size for each p value from each t-map,    accumulate statistics across the 10,000 t-maps, and then determine the cluster-size threshold to achieve a given FPR, for each p value. clustsim_perm <- afni_3dclustsim$new(   residuals_file = "/proj/mnhallqlab/users/michael/mmclock_pe/mmclock_nov2021/feat_l3/L1m-pe/L2m-l2_l2c-overall/L3m-age_sex/FEAT_l1c-EV_pe.gfeat/cope1.feat/stats/res4d.nii.gz",   residuals_mask_file = "/proj/mnhallqlab/users/michael/mmclock_pe/mmclock_nov2021/feat_l3/L1m-pe/L2m-l2_l2c-overall/L3m-age_sex/FEAT_l1c-EV_pe.gfeat/cope1.feat/mask.nii.gz",   residuals_njobs = 32,  scheduler = "slurm", prefix = "res4d", iter = 30000,   clustsim_mask = "/proj/mnhallqlab/lab_resources/standard/mni_icbm152_nlin_asym_09c/mni_icbm152_t1_tal_nlin_asym_09c_mask_2.3mm.nii", ncpus = 8 ) clustsim_perm$submit()  clustsim_perm$is_complete() clustsim_perm$get_clustsim_df() %>% dplyr::filter(nn==1 & pthr==.001) feat_info <- read_feat_dir("/proj/mnhallqlab/users/michael/mmclock_pe/mmclock_nov2021/feat_l3/L1m-pe/L2m-l2_l2c-overall/L3m-age_sex/FEAT_l1c-EV_pe.gfeat/cope1.feat")  feat_info$cope_df %>% select(cope_number, contrast_name, z) cobj <- clustsim_perm$apply_clustsim(   statistic_nifti = "/proj/mnhallqlab/users/michael/mmclock_pe/mmclock_nov2021/feat_l3/L1m-pe/L2m-l2_l2c-overall/L3m-age_sex/FEAT_l1c-EV_pe.gfeat/cope1.feat/stats/zstat6.nii.gz" ) cobj$get_clust_df() cobj$get_outputs()

Although technically correct entire mass statistically significant, can deeply dissatisfying neuroscientist see cluster spans many brains regions functionally /anatomically distinct. ‘mega clusters’ likely reflect co-activation potentially distinct neural circuits/systems. extract betas mega cluster, however, left just one number (usually mean) subject represents neural activity across wide array regions span cluster. possible voxelwise activation pattern indeed homogeneous within mega cluster mean representative. think strong often untested assumption. Moreover, inferences mega clusters often awkward, leading sentence like, “Individuals higher scores working memory test also showed stronger reward prediction activity across parietal occipital cortices.” Perhaps true -subjects association prediction errors working memory spans expanse parietal occipital cortices, yet even plausible strength association may vary region subregion ways mean beta coefficient uncover. mega cluster emerges, intuitive approach increase statistical threshold relative FWE p < .05 level order see mega cluster breaks smaller clusters modulated even strongly (e.g,. FWE p < .005). happens (breaking smaller clusters one raises threshold), can lead greater anatomical specificity still preserving Type error control (indeed, smaller clusters survive stringent correction). title: Considerations for Longitudinal fMRI Analysis in fmri.pipeline
text: Longitudinal fMRI data presents unique statistical challenges, specifically regarding proper treatment variance components across different time points (sessions) versus repeated within-session observations (runs). vignette details current longitudinal pathways fmri.pipeline, highlights specific statistical computational considerations FSL users, shows hybrid FSL L1/L2 + AFNI 3dLMEr L3 workflow preferable. package now supports two practically important longitudinal routes: FSL-native longitudinal analysis, session-level maps modeled Level 3 subject explanatory variables (l3_input_mode = "pooled_sessions_subject_ev"). hybrid workflow FSL produces session-level COPEs Level 2 AFNI 3dLMEr fits longitudinal mixed-effects model Level 3 (l3_input_mode = "3dlmer"). distinction matters. AFNI’s current 3dLMEr documentation describes flexible successor 3dLME, especially complex random-effects structures. fmri.pipeline, however, 3dLMEr used session-level longitudinal modeler top FSL-derived L2 inputs rather single end--end replacement lower-level FSL estimation.

title: Considerations for Longitudinal fMRI Analysis in fmri.pipeline
text: longitudinal studies, practical decision : Separate Sessions: Use l2_scope = "id_session" rather pooling runs subject across visits. FSL Categorical Comparison: Use l3_input_mode = "pooled_sessions_subject_ev" visit structure balanced main goal FSL-native categorical session comparison. Flexible Mixed-Effects: Use l3_input_mode = "3dlmer" visits irregular, follow-incomplete, time continuous, scientific model needs subject-specific random slopes. Contiguous Pseudo-Runs: Reserve l2_scope = "id" cases sessions effectively pseudo-runs longitudinal change parameter interest.

what: Statistical Framework: The Hierarchical fMRI Model
title: Considerations for Longitudinal fMRI Analysis in fmri.pipeline
text: examining specific implementations, useful formalize hierarchical structure underlying multi-run, multi-session fMRI analyses. complete longitudinal fMRI dataset features (least) four nested levels variation: time points within runs, runs within sessions, sessions within subjects, subjects within population. FSL’s architecture, however, provides three modeling stages, forcing one variance components either collapsed ignored.

text: subject ii, session jj, run rr, Level 1 model standard General Linear Model (GLM) applied preprocessed BOLD time series: Yijr=Xijrβijr+ϵijrY_{ijr} = X_{ijr} \beta_{ijr} + \epsilon_{ijr} : YijrY_{ijr} T×1T \times 1 vector BOLD signal given voxel (TT = number time points), XijrX_{ijr} T×pT \times p design matrix pp convolved regressors, βijr\beta_{ijr} p×1p \times 1 vector parameter estimates (PEs), ϵijr∼𝒩(0,σijr2Vijr)\epsilon_{ijr} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_{ijr} V_{ijr}) temporally autocorrelated noise, VijrV_{ijr} captures autocorrelation structure (estimated via FILM prewhitening FSL). outputs Level 1 contrast parameter estimates (COPEs) associated variance COPEs (VARCOPEs), given contrast vector cc: γ̂ijr=cTβ̂ijr,Var(γ̂ijr)=σ̂ijr2cT(XijrTV̂ijr−1Xijr)−1c\hat{\gamma}_{ijr} = c^T \hat{\beta}_{ijr},  \qquad \text{Var}(\hat{\gamma}_{ijr}) = \hat{\sigma}^2_{ijr} \, c^T (X_{ijr}^T \hat{V}_{ijr}^{-1} X_{ijr})^{-1} c VARCOPE critical: carries forward measurement uncertainty run’s time series fit. higher-level analyses FSL condition quantities, treating L1 COPEs “data” L1 VARCOPEs known (estimated) within-run measurement error.

what: Level 2: Combining Runs (Fixed or Mixed Effects)
title: Considerations for Longitudinal fMRI Analysis in fmri.pipeline
text: Level 2, L1 COPEs multiple runs combined. given subject ii session jj (using l2_scope = "id_session"), model : γ̂ijr=zijrTαij+ηijr+eijr\hat{\gamma}_{ijr} = z_{ijr}^T \alpha_{ij} + \eta_{ijr} + e_{ijr} : γ̂ijr\hat{\gamma}_{ijr} L1 COPE run rr (treated “observed data” L2), zijrz_{ijr} L2 design row run rr (often simply 1 averaging, run-specific contrast weight), αij\alpha_{ij} L2 parameter vector interest (e.g., session-level mean activation), ηijr∼𝒩(0,σruns|ij2)\eta_{ijr} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_{\text{runs}|ij}) -run random effect (present using mixed effects; zero fixed effects), eijre_{ijr} known variance Var(γ̂ijr)\text{Var}(\hat{\gamma}_{ijr}), .e., L1 VARCOPE propagated upward. FSL’s fixed effects (FE) approach, default Level 2, -run variance σruns2\sigma^2_{\text{runs}} forced zero, combination reduces inverse-variance weighted averaging: α̂ijFE=∑rwijrγ̂ijr∑rwijr,wijr=1Var(γ̂ijr)\hat{\alpha}_{ij}^{\text{FE}}  = \frac{\sum_r w_{ijr} \, \hat{\gamma}_{ijr}}{\sum_r w_{ijr}}, \qquad w_{ijr} = \frac{1}{\text{Var}(\hat{\gamma}_{ijr})} Var(α̂ijFE)=1∑rwijr\text{Var}(\hat{\alpha}_{ij}^{\text{FE}}) = \frac{1}{\sum_r w_{ijr}} critical assumption runs exchangeable conditional measurement variance: run treated independent replicate underlying signal, differences precision represented L1 VARCOPEs. output VARCOPE L2 reflects propagated L1 uncertainty, genuine -run variability. FSL’s mixed effects (FLAME) approach, total variance run’s COPE becomes: Vartotal(γ̂ijr)=σruns|ij2+Var(γ̂ijr)\text{Var}_{\text{total}}(\hat{\gamma}_{ijr}) = \sigma^2_{\text{runs}|ij} + \text{Var}(\hat{\gamma}_{ijr}) FLAME estimates σruns2\sigma^2_{\text{runs}} data, resulting L2 VARCOPE larger (conservative) incorporates genuine -run heterogeneity.

title: Considerations for Longitudinal fMRI Analysis in fmri.pipeline
text: normal analysis, build pipeline object model specs, let run_glm_pipeline() handle staged setup execution: FSL-native L3 model, set glm_software = "fsl" pass FSL L3 model name, example l3_model_names = "visit_effect_fsl". AFNI 3dLMEr model, L1 L2 stages still run FSL; AFNI begins FSL L2 subject-session COPEs exist. setup preview debugging session, run setup functions explicitly inspect generated setup tables. explicit workflow, level depends outputs previous level: L3 setup needs complete L2 inputs, AFNI 3dLMEr setup harvests FSL L2 subject-session COPEs. face/house deep-dive integration tests use explicit setup-preview style, mocked upstream completions, check paths, model pairing, generated scripts submitting full cluster run.
code: library(fmri.pipeline)  gpa <- setup_glm_pipeline(   analysis_name = "my_longitudinal_study",   scheduler = "slurm",   output_directory = "/path/to/analysis_output",   subject_data = subject_data,   run_data = run_data,   trial_data = trial_data,   glm_software = c("fsl", "afni"),  # use "fsl" only for the FSL-native route   n_expected_runs = 2L )  gpa <- build_l1_models(gpa, from_spec_file = "l1_models.yaml") gpa <- build_l2_models(gpa, from_spec_file = "l2_models.yaml") gpa <- build_l3_models(gpa, from_spec_file = "l3_models.yaml")  run_glm_pipeline(   gpa,   l1_model_names = "facehouse",   l2_model_names = "session_summary",   l3_model_names = "visit_effect_3dlmer" ) gpa <- setup_l1_models(gpa, l1_model_names = "facehouse")  # Run L1, or refresh status after L1 outputs already exist. # Then prepare the per-subject-session L2 models. gpa <- setup_l2_models(   gpa,   l1_model_names = "facehouse",   l2_model_names = "session_summary",   backend = "fsl" )  # Run L2, or refresh status after L2 outputs already exist. # Then prepare L3 from the completed FSL L2 subject-session COPEs. gpa <- setup_l3_models(   gpa,   l1_model_names = "facehouse",   l2_model_names = "session_summary",   l3_model_names = "visit_effect_3dlmer",   backend = "afni" )

what: The Challenge of Variance in FSL
title: Considerations for Longitudinal fMRI Analysis in fmri.pipeline
text: FSL’s standard higher-level analysis tool, FLAME (FMRIB’s Local Analysis Mixed Effects), designed intrinsically around hierarchical fixed mixed-effects variance modeling. cross-sectional data multiple runs per subject, standard pipeline : 1. Level 1 (Run): General Linear Model (GLM) time series data. 2. Level 2 (Subject): Fixed-effects combination multiple runs. 3. Level 3 (Group): Mixed-effects (FLAME 1/2) combination across subjects. additional level, session wave, introduced, researchers must decide fold extra temporal dimension FSL’s rigid 3-level structure. FSL generally struggles true repeated-measures modeling, observations non-independent due within-subject clustering. mitigate , fmri.pipeline offers workarounds.

text: Violation Exchangeable Variance: FSL’s fixed-effects combination L2 assumes error variance input copes conceptually equivalent exchangeable. Pooling runs entirely different sessions forces FSL assume variability two runs within session mathematically identical variance two runs across sessions (e.g., separated 1 year). rarely true due state versus trait variances. Efficiency Computation: Generating L2 analysis high number inputs (e.g., 4 runs x 3 sessions = 12 inputs) significantly bottlenecks FLAME estimation. Fragility: single bad run Session 1 detected, entire multi-session L2 model subject must deleted recomputed, costing substantial wall-time compute clusters.

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: fmri.pipeline uses fixed three-level vocabulary: Level 1: Run-level time-series GLM. Events parametric modulators convolved HRF estimated separately run. Level 2: Within-subject run combination. Multiple Level 1 COPEs combined within subject subject-session. Level 3: Sample-level group-level model. Subject/session maps modeled across participants. single-run task, standalone Level 2 run-combination step. pipeline still calls group model Level 3 nomenclature stable across single-run multi-run analyses.

code: gpa <- setup_glm_pipeline(   analysis_name = "memory_task",   trial_data = trial_df,   run_data = run_df,   subject_data = subject_df,   tr = 0.9,   vm = c(     id = "participant_id",     session = "visit",     run_number = "run",     trial = "trial_index",     run_nifti = "bold_file"   ) )

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: trial_data one row per trial per run. provides event timing trial-varying variables used build Level 1 regressors. Typical columns include: id, session, run_number, trial onset columns, seconds start run, cue_onset feedback_onset duration columns, seconds, cue_duration feedback_duration intertrial/interstimulus interval columns used truncation logic model specification parametric modulators, prediction error, reaction time, value, uncertainty within-run condition factors, condition feedback_type Example: Onsets durations seconds. event constant duration, can either include constant duration column specify fixed duration model specification.
code: trial_df <- tibble::tibble(   id = c("10637", "10637", "10637"),   session = 1,   run_number = 1,   trial = 1:3,   cue_onset = c(8.07, 15.20, 19.73),   cue_duration = c(1.05, 2.45, 2.25),   feedback_onset = c(9.19, 17.72, 22.05),   feedback_duration = 0.9,   iti_duration = c(5.1, 1.1, 2.1),   condition = c("fear", "fear", "happy"),   prediction_error = c(26.28, 54.05, 33.34) )

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: run_data one row per fMRI acquisition. connects task design preprocessed BOLD files run-level covariates. robust approach provide run_nifti directly. run_nifti absent, pipeline can search subject_data$mr_dir using fmri_file_regex, fmri_path_regex, run_number_regex, explicit paths easier debug. Typical columns include: id, session, run_number run_nifti, path preprocessed 4D NIfTI run confound_input_file, path confound TSV/TXT file, often fMRIPrep postprocessing tr, TR varies across runs run-level predictors Level 2 models, condition, difficulty, scanner Example:
code: run_df <- tibble::tibble(   id = c("10637", "10637"),   session = 1,   run_number = c(1, 2),   condition = c("fear", "happy"),   run_nifti = c(     "/proj/lab/derivatives/sub-10637/func/sub-10637_task-clock_run-1_desc-preproc_bold.nii.gz",     "/proj/lab/derivatives/sub-10637/func/sub-10637_task-clock_run-2_desc-preproc_bold.nii.gz"   ),   confound_input_file = c(     "/proj/lab/derivatives/sub-10637/func/sub-10637_task-clock_run-1_desc-confounds_timeseries.tsv",     "/proj/lab/derivatives/sub-10637/func/sub-10637_task-clock_run-2_desc-confounds_timeseries.tsv"   ) )

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: subject_data one row per subject-session. provides Level 3 covariates subject/session metadata. Typical columns include: id session group indicators, demographics, clinical variables, behavioral scores session-level variables longitudinal analyses, visit label days since baseline Example: longitudinal designs, store one row per id session, one row per subject:
code: subject_df <- tibble::tibble(   id = c("10637", "10638", "10711"),   session = 1,   age = c(16.70, 16.21, 18.32),   sex = c("M", "M", "F"),   group = c("control", "control", "patient") ) subject_df <- tibble::tibble(   id = c("10637", "10637", "10638", "10638"),   session = c(1, 2, 1, 2),   session_label = c("baseline", "followup", "baseline", "followup"),   days_since_baseline = c(0, 365, 0, 391),   age_baseline = c(16.70, 16.70, 16.21, 16.21) )

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: derivatives close BIDS/fMRIPrep layout, helper functions can create starter tables: Treat starting point. Always inspect result join analysis-specific task variables running pipeline.
code: run_df <- generate_run_data_from_bids(   bids_dir = "/proj/lab/derivatives/fmriprep",   task_name = "clock",   desc = "preproc",   suffix = "bold",   space = "MNI152NLin2009cAsym" )  subject_df <- generate_subject_data_from_bids(   bids_dir = "/proj/lab/derivatives/fmriprep" )  trial_df <- generate_trial_data_from_bids(   bids_dir = "/proj/lab/derivatives/fmriprep",   task_name = "clock" )

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: setup_glm_pipeline() validates inputs, normalizes column names, sets output locations, records scheduler compute settings, stores model specifications. models available yet, pass NULL build afterward. Key setup arguments: analysis_name: stable name analysis. output_directory already end name, pipeline appends . scheduler: "slurm", "torque", "local". Use "local" small tests. tr: repetition time seconds. TR differs run, provide tr column run_data instead. drop_volumes: number leading volumes remove timing/confound alignment. n_expected_runs: used warnings subject/run completeness checks. confound_settings: tells pipeline nuisance regressors include, volumes spike, exclude truncate runs. parallel: controls setup cores scheduler time/memory settings. confound expressions evaluated run-level confound data motion regressors framewise_displacement prepared. Expressions can refer confound columns plus helper variables time, volume, last_onset, last_offset, last_isi.
code: library(fmri.pipeline) library(dplyr)  trial_df <- readr::read_csv("inputs/clock_trials.csv") run_df <- readr::read_csv("inputs/clock_runs.csv") subject_df <- readr::read_csv("inputs/clock_subjects.csv")  gpa <- setup_glm_pipeline(   analysis_name = "clock_glm_2026_05_11",   scheduler = "slurm",   output_directory = "/proj/lab/analysis/clock",   trial_data = trial_df,   run_data = run_df,   subject_data = subject_df,   tr = 0.9,   drop_volumes = 2,   n_expected_runs = 2,   l1_models = NULL,   l2_models = NULL,   l3_models = NULL,   glm_software = "fsl",   confound_settings = list(     confound_input_colnames = NULL,     l1_confound_regressors = c(       "csf", "csf_derivative1",       "white_matter", "white_matter_derivative1"     ),     na_strings = c("NA", "n/a", ""),     spike_volumes = "framewise_displacement > 0.9",     exclude_run = "max(framewise_displacement, na.rm = TRUE) > 5 || mean(framewise_displacement > 0.9, na.rm = TRUE) > 0.10",     exclude_subject = "n_good_runs < 1",     truncate_run = "(framewise_displacement > 0.9 & time > last_offset) | (time > last_offset + last_isi)"   ),   parallel = list(     l1_setup_cores = 4L,     pipeline_cores = "default",     finalize_time = "2:00:00",     fsl = list(       l1_feat_alljobs_time = "72:00:00",       l2_feat_time = "4:00:00",       l3_feat_time = "24:00:00"     )   ),   lgr_threshold = "info" )

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: Models can built interactively read YAML/JSON. reproducible analysis, recommended workflow : Build model interactively , useful. Export export_glm_config(). Edit YAML project-owned specification. Rebuild future gpa objects specification.

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: can also ask setup_glm_pipeline() prompt immediately using l1_models = "prompt", l2_models = "prompt", l3_models = "prompt".
code: gpa <- build_l1_models(gpa) gpa <- build_l2_models(gpa) gpa <- build_l3_models(gpa)  export_glm_config(gpa, file = "clock_glm_spec.yaml")

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: YAML file can contain L1, L2, L3 model definitions: compact example defines two events, two unit-height signals, one parametric signal, run-average Level 2 model, intercept-Level 3 model: Model concepts: Events define something happened: onset, duration, optional ISI/ITI. Signals define regressors estimate: event plus amplitude/modulation HRF normalization. Parametric modulators continuous signal amplitudes. default, design-matrix generation centers values (additional$bdm_args$center_values = TRUE), makes unit-height event regressors parametric effects easier interpret together. L1 models choose signals enter run-level GLM L1 contrasts created. L2 models combine runs within subject subject-session. Use l2_scope: id_session ordinary within-session run combination longitudinal workflows. Use l2_scope: id pooling sessions inside subject scientifically intended. L3 models define group inference. multi-session data, l3_input_mode controls session-level maps feed Level 3. Contrasts contrasts: control diagonal COPEs, condition means, pairwise differences, cell means, overall responses. Keeping contrast settings nested contrasts: matches exported configs. guide shows minimal contrast settings. full explanation diagonal contrasts, condition means, pairwise differences, cell means, overall response, simple slopes, emmeans weighting, custom contrast entry, see vignette("interactive-model-builder"). categorical predictors Level 2 Level 3, include reference levels interpretation matters: covariate_transform: age: mean mean-centers age model construction. Centering continuous covariates usually makes intercept condition means easier interpret.
code: gpa <- build_l1_models(gpa, from_spec_file = "clock_glm_spec.yaml") gpa <- build_l2_models(gpa, from_spec_file = "clock_glm_spec.yaml") gpa <- build_l3_models(gpa, from_spec_file = "clock_glm_spec.yaml") onsets:   - cue_onset   - feedback_onset  durations:   - cue_duration   - feedback_duration  isis:   - iti_duration  values:   - prediction_error  wi_factors:   - condition  events:   cue:     onset: cue_onset     duration: cue_duration     isi: iti_duration    feedback:     onset: feedback_onset     duration: feedback_duration     isi: iti_duration  signals:   cue:     event: cue     value_fixed: 1     normalization: none    feedback:     event: feedback     value_fixed: 1     normalization: none    pe_feedback:     event: feedback     parametric_modulator: prediction_error     normalization: evtmax_1  l1_models:   task:     signals:       - cue       - feedback       - pe_feedback     contrasts:       diagonal: yes  l2_models:   run_average:     level: 2     model_formula: ~ 1     l2_scope: id_session     contrasts:       diagonal: yes       overall_response: yes  l3_models:   group_mean:     level: 3     model_formula: ~ 1     l3_input_mode: per_session     contrasts:       diagonal: yes       overall_response: yes     fsl_outlier_deweighting: no l3_models:   group_by_age:     level: 3     model_formula: ~ group + age     reference_level:       group: control     covariate_transform:       age: mean     contrasts:       diagonal: yes       cond_means: group       pairwise_diffs: group       overall_response: yes     fsl_outlier_deweighting: no

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: standard FSL analysis, special backend configuration needed: different software different levels, use gpa$level_backends: model-specific control, set backend fields model object YAML. current recommended pattern AFNI 3dLMEr Level 3 models consume FSL-produced Level 2 inputs: Current limitations important: FSL supports separate Level 1, Level 2, Level 3 execution. SPM support uses SPM-style run/session handling run standalone Level 2 FEAT-style step. AFNI 3dLMEr currently supported Level 3 execution backend. Level 3 producer inputs currently implemented FSL. practice, AFNI Level 3 usually specify producer_backend: fsl. run_glm_pipeline() starts, resolves backend selection, validates producer requirements, logs backend preflight report. see backend producer error, read report first.
code: gpa$glm_software <- "fsl" gpa$glm_software <- "fsl" gpa$level_backends <- list(   l1 = "fsl",   l2 = "fsl",   l3 = "afni" ) l2_models:   session_average:     level: 2     model_formula: ~ 1     l2_scope: id_session     contrasts:       diagonal: yes       overall_response: yes  l3_models:   longitudinal_lmer:     level: 3     model_formula: ~ session_label     l3_input_mode: 3dlmer     execution_backend: afni     producer_backend: fsl     random_effects: "(1 | Subj)"     contrasts:       cond_means: session_label       pairwise_diffs: session_label       overall_response: yes

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: Submit full selected analysis : default, run_glm_pipeline() prompts choose L1, L2, L3 models. scripted runs, pass model names explicitly: can also apply temporary backend overrides run time: submitted job sequence usually: finalize_configuration: checks compute environment, validates models, initializes SQLite tracker, looks NIfTI metadata, finalizes defaults. setup_l1: creates Level 1 design matrices, timing files, backend-specific setup files FSL .fsf files. run_l1_<backend>: submits waits run-level model jobs. setup_run_l2_<backend>: sets runs Level 2 jobs standalone Level 2 step needed. setup_run_l3_<backend>: sets runs Level 3 jobs. cleanup_glm: refreshes status tables reconciles backend-specific caches. exact steps depend selected models, whether task single-run multi-run, backends requested.
code: run_glm_pipeline(gpa) run_glm_pipeline(   gpa,   l1_model_names = "task",   l2_model_names = "run_average",   l3_model_names = "group_mean" ) run_glm_pipeline(   gpa,   l1_model_names = "task",   l2_model_names = "session_average",   l3_model_names = "longitudinal_lmer",   level_backends = list(l3 = "afni") )

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: analysis top-level output directory, usually: Important contents include: Default FEAT paths include model contrast labels avoid collisions: output templates stored gpa$output_locations use glue syntax. Advanced users can override running pipeline, new users start defaults.
code: <output_directory>/<analysis_name>/ feat_l1/                 # FSL Level 1 setup files and .feat outputs feat_l2/                 # FSL Level 2 .fsf files and .gfeat outputs feat_l3/                 # FSL Level 3 .fsf files and .gfeat outputs afni_3dlmer/             # AFNI 3dLMEr scripts and outputs, when AFNI is used logs/                    # text and JSON logs from setup functions scheduler_scripts/       # one batch_<uuid>/ directory per run_glm_pipeline() call project_config.json      # analysis metadata and batch history *.sqlite                 # SQLite job-tracking database feat_l1/sub-<id>/ses-<session>/<l1_model>/FEAT_LVL1_run<run_number>.feat feat_l2/sub-<id>/ses-<session>/L1m-<l1_model>/l1c-<l1_cope_label>/L2m-<l2_model>.gfeat feat_l3/L3m-<l3_model>/L1m-<l1_model>/L2m-<l2_model>/l2c-<l2_contrast>/FEAT_l1c-<l1_contrast>.gfeat

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: easiest interactive route : scripted checks, inspect latest batch directory SQLite job tracker. Load run cache inspect model status: Backend-specific caches, run_pipeline_cache_fsl.RData run_pipeline_cache_afni.RData, can informative saved immediately backend-specific jobs finish: SLURM, scheduler exit codes often fastest way distinguish model errors time memory kills: FSL outputs, successful artifacts contain .feat_complete contain .feat_fail. Current pipeline runs also write job_manifest.tsv artifact directories, linking result back scheduler job, batch script, scheduler log produced :
code: diagnose_pipeline(gpa) batch_dirs <- list.dirs(   gpa$output_locations$scheduler_scripts,   recursive = FALSE,   full.names = TRUE ) latest_batch <- batch_dirs[which.max(file.info(batch_dirs)$mtime)] latest_batch load(file.path(latest_batch, "run_pipeline_cache.RData"))  if (!is.null(gpa$l1_model_setup$fsl)) {   gpa$l1_model_setup$fsl |>     dplyr::count(feat_complete, feat_failed, to_run) }  if (!is.null(gpa$l2_model_setup$fsl)) {   gpa$l2_model_setup$fsl |>     dplyr::filter(feat_failed | !feat_complete) |>     dplyr::select(id, session, l1_model, l1_cope_name, l2_model, feat_dir, feat_fsf) }  if (!is.null(gpa$l3_model_setup$fsl)) {   gpa$l3_model_setup$fsl |>     dplyr::filter(feat_failed | !feat_complete) |>     dplyr::select(l1_model, l2_model, l3_model, feat_dir, feat_fsf) }  if (!is.null(gpa$l3_model_setup$afni)) {   gpa$l3_model_setup$afni |>     dplyr::filter(afni_failed | !afni_complete) |>     dplyr::select(l3_model, l1_cope_name, l2_cope_name, output_file, afni_script) } load(file.path(latest_batch, "run_pipeline_cache_fsl.RData")) gpa$l2_model_setup$fsl |>   dplyr::filter(feat_failed | !feat_complete) sacct -j 49616891,49616892 --format=JobID,JobName%45,State,ExitCode,Elapsed,MaxRSS manifest <- read.delim(   "/path/to/analysis/feat_l2/sub-10637/ses-1/L1m-task/l1c-EV_feedback/L2m-run_average.gfeat/job_manifest.tsv",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  manifest[, c("status", "job_id", "job_name", "scheduler_log", "batch_file")]

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: overlapping IDs across tables: check id coding trial_data, run_data, subject_data. pipeline coerces id character, fix inconsistent IDs. Duplicated subject rows: subject_data must one row per id session. Missing run files: prefer explicit run_nifti paths verify file.exists(run_df$run_nifti) setup. TR provided: provide either tr = <seconds> setup_glm_pipeline() numeric tr column run_data. Model specs finalized: run_glm_pipeline() requires valid l1_model_set. Build L1 models submission. Confound column mismatch: confound files header, set confound_input_colnames. use fMRIPrep-style missing values, set na_strings inside confound_settings. Backend mismatch: AFNI Level 3 currently expects l3_input_mode: 3dlmer FSL-produced upstream inputs.

title: fmri.pipeline quick start guide
text: first real project, start one L1 model, one L2 model, one intercept-L3 model. path submits status checks clean, add scientific contrasts, covariates, alternate backend models.
code: library(fmri.pipeline)  trial_df <- readr::read_csv("inputs/trials.csv") run_df <- readr::read_csv("inputs/runs.csv") subject_df <- readr::read_csv("inputs/subjects.csv")  stopifnot(all(file.exists(run_df$run_nifti)))  gpa <- setup_glm_pipeline(   analysis_name = "task_glm",   scheduler = "slurm",   output_directory = "/proj/lab/analysis",   trial_data = trial_df,   run_data = run_df,   subject_data = subject_df,   tr = 0.9,   drop_volumes = 2,   n_expected_runs = 2,   l1_models = NULL,   l2_models = NULL,   l3_models = NULL,   glm_software = "fsl" )  gpa <- build_l1_models(gpa, from_spec_file = "task_glm_spec.yaml") gpa <- build_l2_models(gpa, from_spec_file = "task_glm_spec.yaml") gpa <- build_l3_models(gpa, from_spec_file = "task_glm_spec.yaml")  run_glm_pipeline(   gpa,   l1_model_names = "task",   l2_model_names = "run_average",   l3_model_names = "group_mean" )  diagnose_pipeline(gpa)

title: Analysis of fMRI Data on HPC clusters
text: fsl_model_arguments file gets saved run_fsl_pipeline.R critical input subsidiary scripts pipeline additional analysis scripts. structure follows: analysis name: string used name resulting group analysis folder. Set something describes overall dataset, preprocessing approach, model-based input. trial_statistics: long-form data.frame containing trial statistics subject analyzed. Trials subjects stacked rows, leading data.frame subjects x trials total rows. data.frame used calculate model-based fMRI regressors setup GLM design matrices subject. subject_covariates: data.frame containing covariates used group analysis. one row per subject. id_col: string specifying column name subject ID data provided input (esp. subject_covariates trial_statistics). used merge data together needed. fmri_dir: root path preprocessed data entire project expectdir: subfolder name within fmri_dir containing preprocessed fMRI data given subject. expectfile: regular expression denoting expected filename preprocessed fMRI data entered first-level GLMs. usepreconvolve: boolean indicating whether regressors convolved HRF prior entry FSL FEAT. Convolution achieved using build_design_matrix function dependlab R package. recommend TRUE, better tested. use FALSE, pipeline output -called three-column timing files (onset, duration, parametric value), entered FEAT, convolution occurs within FEAT. ncpus: controls many CPUs used running FEAT level 2 analysis (OLS combination runs). probably rename clarity. drop_volumes: controls number initial volumes dropped run fMRI data GLM analysis. Useful eliminating volumes steady state magnetization. tr: repetition time (seconds) fMRI scan sequence. important various temporal filtering steps applied correctly. spikeregressors: boolean controlling whether 1/0 regressors large head movement added confound regressors GLM. Handled downstream model_clock_fmri. l1_model_variants: list element vector signals included simultaneously level 1 analysis. element list, level 1 data analyzed requested combination regressors, group models combination also conducted. Thus, total number models product l1_model_variants (level 1) group_model_variants (level 3). group_model_variants: list element vector covariates included group analysis. names refer columns subject_covariates data.frame. l1_contrasts: named list whose elements lists specifying set level 1 contrasts included. name vector used cross-reference corresponding name l1_model_variants. example, model named ‘m1’ l1_model_variants, can add level 1 contrasts model using ‘m1’ name list l1_contrasts. execute_feat: boolean indicating whether execute FEAT level 1 analysis creating FSF file. somewhat vestigial pipeline variant run desktop computer. cluster-based execution, FALSE, creates FSF file, hands execution scheduler. model_suffix: optional string appended folder names output level 1 analyses specified l1_model_variants. useful running two models level 1 regressors, model nevertheless somewhat different (e.g., different contrasts). root_workdir: location temporary job scripts generated run model. cleaned pipeline completes (since distribted), can deleted manually don’t need . n_cluster_beta_cpus: number CPUs used simultaneously extract regression coefficients (betas) single runs/subjects based significant group-level clusters. badids: optional vector IDs excluded analysis. dorpped pipeline begins. zthresh: minimum z-stastistic threshold used identifying whole-brain-significant clusters extraction scripts. passed, defaults |z| > 3.09. clustsize: minimum number voxels zthresh must present cluster retained extraction. second component cluster-based thresholding approach whole-brain significance (e.g., using 3dClustSim). specified, defaults 34.

text: parent_jobs vector parent_jobs upstream job may influence execution depends_on_parents logical character string indicating whether job wait parent_jobs complete. character string passed, indicates named elements parent_jobs must complete job begins. job_name user-defined name job used specifying job dependencies informative job status queries job scheduler wait_for_children TRUE, code inserted wait jobs vector called child_job_ids finish batch exits. code use variable name all_children_successful TRUE, jobs vector child_job_ids must successful job finish (see wait_for_children field) wall_time amount time requested job scheduler, following d-hh:mm:ss format. Defaults "4:00:00", 4 hours. n_nodes number nodes requested job scheduler n_cpus number cores (aka 'cpus', ignoring hyperthreading) requested job scheduler mem_total total amount memory (RAM) requested job mem_per_cpu amount memory (RAM) requested per cpu (total = mem_per_cpu * n_cpus) input_objects environment containing objects written RData object passed batch job execution input_rdata_file name environment loaded beginning R batch prior executing output_rdata_file name environment saved end R batch execution, can loaded subsequent jobs. sqlite_db File path tracking SQLite database batch_directory Location batch scripts written batch_code Shell code included batch script prior R code run. can include module load statements, environment variable exports, etc. r_code R code executed scheduler. can character vector includes multiple R statements expression object containing R code evaluated post_children_r_code R code executed child jobs completed. can character vector includes multiple R statements expression object containing R code evaluated. relevant wait_for_children = TRUE r_packages R packages loaded environment job execution. loaded pacman::p_load, install missing packages attempting load scheduler job scheduler used batch. Options : "slurm", "torque", "local". scheduler_options optional character vector scheduler arguments included batch script header control additional features job emails group permissions. directives added #SBATCH #PBS headings, depending scheduler, ignored scheduler "local". repolling_interval number seconds wait successive checks whether parent jobs completed. mostly relevant 'local' scheduler. print_session_info TRUE, print `sessionInfo()` `Sys.info()` job starts. Useful debugging problems compute environment R installation. print_environment `TRUE`, print `Sys.getenv()` job starts. can produce lot output, can useful certain environment variables found job runs, leading fail. sqlite_cache_obj `TRUE`, copy object SQLite database submission time. mostly useful detailed debugging generally recommended can increase size database considerably

title: wrapper class for 3dClusterize — afni_3dclusterize
text: inset 4D dataset containing statistic use thresholding (ithr) , optionally, data value output/retain mask specified, volume masked mask prior clusterizing threshold_file 3D dataset containing statistic use thresholding. Mutually exclusive inset passed, ithr idat ignored inset file generated internally. data_file 3D dataset containing data value retained clusters post-thresholding. Must passed threshold_file stitched together internally. Mutually exclusive inset. mask_from_hdr passes -mask_from_hdr out_mask passes -out_mask ithr sub-brik number voxelwise threshold. Passes -ithr idat sub-brik number voxelwise data output cluster table. Passes -idat onesided TRUE, clusterizing conducted one tail statistic distribution (-ithr) twosided TRUE, clusterizing conducted tails statistic distribution (-ithr) bisided TRUE, clusterizing conducted tail distribution individually lower_thresh lower tail cutoff two/bi-sided testing upper_thresh upper tail cutoff two/bi-sided testing one_thresh threshold value one-sided testing one_tail one-sided clusterizing, whether threshold LEFT RIGHT tail distribution NN 1, 2, 3. Default: 1. Passes -NN. clust_nvox minimum number voxels allowed cluster. Passes -clust_nvol clust_vol minimum volume (microliters) allowed cluster (mutually exclusive clust_nvox). Passes -clust_vol pref_map File name integer-valued mask containing cluster, ordered descending voxel size. Passes -pref_map. pref_dat File name clusterized thresholded data. Passes -pref_dat. quiet passes -quiet. orient passes -orient. 'RAI' 'LPI'. Default LPI. binary TRUE, pref_map (cluster mask) output 1/0 binary image instead integer-valued. Passes -binary. clusterize_output_file name/location 3dClusterize output file containing table identified clusters. Defaults adding suffix '_clusters.1D' input image placing file folder input.

text: out_dir intended output directory 3dClustSim files prefix prefix included names 3dClustSim output files fwhmx_input_files character vector input files passed 3dFWHMx (-ACF method) fwhmx_mask_files character vector masks containing volume estimate ACF 3dFWHMx. Must match 1:1 fwhmx_input_files. residuals_file filename group residuals file used null dataset generation (permutation approach). residuals_mask_file volume null datasets generated residuals residuals_njobs number independent jobs splitting residuals permutations. NULL, 32 jobs used. inset_files character vector dataset files pass directly 3dClustSim via -inset. insdat_file sdat file containing null datasets passed 3dClustSim via -insdat. insdat_mask_file mask file corresponding insdat_file maps values space. dxyz size voxels x y z (vector 3 numbers) nxyz number voxels along x y z (vector 3 positive integers) clustsim_mask controls volume correct FWE using 3dClustSim. give whole-brain mask, cluster thresholds reflect whole-brain FWE correction. give smaller mask (e.g., single region network), correcting volume (.e., small-volume correction). acf_params vector 3 autocorrelation parameters (, b, c) used simulate smoothness. Usually produced 3dFWHMx. '' parameter must 0 1. 'b' 'c' parameters (scale radii) must positive. spatial autocorrelation function given : `ACF(r) = * exp(-r*r/(2*b*b)) + (1-)*exp(-r/c)` nopad TRUE, disable 3dClustSim's default 'padding' slices along face allow edge effects smoothing process. Default: FALSE pthr vector voxelwise p-values tested 3dClustSim (-pthr). Can space-separated string numeric vector. Default: ".01 .005 .002 .001 .0005 .0002 .0001". athr vector cluster p-values tested 3dClustSim (-athr). Can space-separated string numeric vector. Default: ".05 .02 .01 .005 .002 .001 .0005 .0002 .0001". iter number iterations use simulating null datasets 3dClustSim. Default: 30000. nodec TRUE, clusters printed without decimal places, rounding (e.g., 27.2 becomes 28). Default: FALSE. seed seed use starting 3dClustSim random number generation. Default: 0 (sets random seed) scheduler HPC scheduler use. Can 'local', 'slurm', 'torque'. ncpus number cores use 3dClustSim job. sets OMP_NUM_THREADS 3dClustSim job speed computation.

Default: "geometric". ncpus number threads/cores use running 3dFWHMx. Default 1. Controls OMP_NUM_THREADS.

Values data.frame run number (1:n), trial (1:x), signal. tr repetition time fMRI sequence seconds. must specify . important specify correctly get temporal filtering correct. center_values logical (TRUE/FALSE) indicating whether center parameteric regressors prior convolution. usually good idea remove collinearity parametric task indicator regressors. default TRUE. hrf_parameters named vector HRF parameters passed fmri.stimulus internally.           parameters a1, a2, b1, b2, cc. Equation (x/d1)^a1 * exp(-(x - d1)/b1) - c * (x/d2)^a2 * exp(-(x - d2)/b2).           Defaults descriptions :             a1 = 6. Controls time delay peak positive response             a2 = 12. Controls time delay (negative) peak undershoot             b1 = 0.9. Controls dispersion (breadth) positive response             b2 = 0.9. Controls dispersion (breadth) undershoot             cc = 0.35. Controls relative scaling positive response versus undershoot. Note: defaults come Glover 1999. Note. FSL double gamma a1 = 6, a2 = 16, cc = 1/6. yet sure b1 b2. baseline_coef_order Default -1 (baseline). >= 0, design include polynomial trends within run (e.g. baseline_coef_order = 1 includes intercept linear trend regressors) baseline_parameterization Defaults "Legendre". adds Legendre polynomials baseline_coef_order (e.g., 2). alternative "orthogonal_polynomials", uses fmri.design fmri package (now internal fmri.pipeline) add polynomial regressors orthogonal substantive design factors. run_data data.frame containing metadata runs want model task design. data.frame contain columns run_number, run_volumes, run_nifti, drop_volumes. run_nifti provided, run_volumes , number volumes looked NIfTI header. run_nifti_drop_applied Logical indicating whether NIfTI inputs run_data$run_nifti already drop_volumes removed. text: list containing different aspects design matrix: $design: runs x signals list containing events convolution.          element 2-D matrix containing, minimally, "trial", onset", "duration", "value" columns.          Onsets durations specified seconds, consistent FSL's 3-column format.          Within matrix, onset, duration value signal specified. $design_convolved: convolved design matrices run. element list contains          run. Within design matrix, column contains regressor, encompassing substantive regressors,          additional signals, polynomial baseline regressors, specified. row reflects predicted value volume. $design_unconvolved: unconvolved design matrices run. structure $design_convolved,          prior convolution HRF. $collin_events: list containing information collinearity regressors convolution.          highest level list, element contains run. second level list,          first element contains correlation matrix regressors second element provides          variance inflation factor (VIF) associated regressor. Example: design$collin_events$run1$vif $collin_convolved: list containing information collinearity convolved regressors,          including substantive signals, additional regressors, polynomial regressors. Follows structure $collin_events. $concat_onsets: list containing concatenated event onset times signal. signal element list containing          vector onset times across runs. , total time run1 added onsets run2 support combined analysis          runs, common AFNI (e.g., using -concat multiple files -input). $run_volumes: vector containing total number volumes modeled run. $design_plot: ggplot object showing design matrix. generated visualize_design_matrix.

title: Creates an fmri design matrix, including timing files for for AFNI or FSL. — build_design_matrix
text: Michael Hallquist Alison Schreiber

title: Creates an fmri design matrix, including timing files for for AFNI or FSL. — build_design_matrix
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   data(example_events)   data(example_signals)    #basic convolved design matrix   d <- build_design_matrix(events = example_events, signals = example_signals, tr=1.0, plot=FALSE)    data(example_nuisrun1) #load demo additional signals   data(example_nuisrun2)    #design matrix with 0,1,2 polynomial baseline regressors and a set of additional regressors   #this does not contain a 'taskness' regressor for cue or outcome   dnuis <- build_design_matrix(events = example_events, signals = example_signals, tr=1.0,     additional_regressors = list(example_nuisrun1, example_nuisrun2), baseline_coef_order = 2)    #tweak the design to add temporal derivatives for both ev and pe   example_signals$pe$add_deriv <- TRUE   example_signals$ev$add_deriv <- TRUE    #also use the evtmax_1 normalization method for both parametric signals   example_signals$pe$normalization <- "evtmax_1"   example_signals$ev$normalization <- "durmax_1"    #finally, add a taskness regressor for cue and outcome   example_signals$cue_evt <- list(value=1, event="cue", normalization="none")   example_signals$outcome_evt <- list(value=1, event="outcome", normalization="none")    #include up to quadratic drift terms in the design, drop 3 volumes from the beginning,   #and write timing files in AFNI, FSL, and convolved formats (to the "run_timing" directory).   d_modified <- build_design_matrix(events = example_events, signals = example_signals, tr=1.0,     baseline_coef_order=2, drop_volumes=3, write_timing_files = c("convolved", "AFNI", "FSL"))    #show unconvolved design   plot(visualize_design_matrix(concat_design_runs(d_modified, convolved=FALSE)))    #beta series approach for cues: 1 regressor per cue event   example_signals$cue_evt$beta_series <- TRUE   example_signals$ev <- NULL   example_signals$pe <- NULL    d_beta <- build_design_matrix(events = example_events, signals = example_signals, tr=1.0, plot=FALSE,     baseline_coef_order=2, drop_volumes=3, write_timing_files = c("convolved", "FSL"))    #PPI example    events <- rbind(     data.frame(event="cue", run_number=1, trial=1:5, onset=c(5, 20, 50, 100, 150), duration=4),     data.frame(event="cue", run_number=2, trial=1:5, onset=c(15, 35, 60, 90, 105), duration=4)   )    signals <- list(     load=list(value=rbind(       data.frame(run_number=1, trial=1:5, value=1:5),       data.frame(run_number=2, trial=1:5, value=1:5)     ), event="cue", normalization="none"),     cue_evt=list(value=1, event="cue", normalization="none"),     cue_evt_ppi=list(value=1, event="cue", normalization="none", ts_multipliers="vs")   )    library(neuRosim)   forppi1 <- simTSrestingstate(nscan=328, base=1, TR=1, SNR=6)   forppi2 <- simTSrestingstate(nscan=242, base=1, TR=1, SNR=6)   test_ppi <- list(run1=data.frame(vs=forppi1), run2=data.frame(vs=forppi2))    #In PPI, the time series itself should typically be included as a regressor   d <- build_design_matrix(events = events, signals = signals, tr=0.5, center_values=TRUE,                 write_timing_files = c("convolved", "AFNI", "FSL"),                 drop_volumes = 0, baseline_coef_order = 2, run_volumes = c(328, 242),                 ts_multipliers = test_ppi, additional_regressors = test_ppi) } # }

what: Small utility function to handle any NAs in the regressor specification. Also, optionally, removes values of 0 from the regressor prior to convolution. The latter is usually a good idea to avoid a non-event (zero) becoming an event after mean centering of a parametric regressor. Convolving a zero-height regressor with an HRF will maintain all zeros, whereas a non-zero value (resulting from mean centering) will not. — cleanup_regressor

title: This function submits a single script to a high-performance cluster using a scheduler (Slurm or TORQUE). It accepts a vector of arguments to be passed to the scheduler and a vector of environment variables that should be passed to the compute node at job execution. — cluster_job_submit
code: cluster_job_submit(   script,   scheduler = "slurm",   sched_args = NULL,   env_variables = NULL,   export_all = FALSE,   echo = TRUE,   fail_on_error = FALSE,   wait_jobs = NULL,   wait_signal = "afterok",   repolling_interval = 60,   tracking_sqlite_db = NULL,   tracking_args = list() )

title: This function submits a single script to a high-performance cluster using a scheduler (Slurm or TORQUE). It accepts a vector of arguments to be passed to the scheduler and a vector of environment variables that should be passed to the compute node at job execution. — cluster_job_submit
text: script script executed scheduler. can contain scheduler directives, case conflicts, directives passed sched_args take precedence. scheduler scheduler use job submission. Options 'qsub', 'torque', 'sbatch', 'slurm', 'sh'. terms 'qsub' 'torque' aliases ('torque' submits via qsub command). Likewise 'sbatch' 'slurm'. scheduler 'sh' submit scheduler , instead executes command immediately via sh. sched_args character vector arguments included scheduling command. TORQUE, typically begin '-l' '-l wall_time=10:00:00'. env_variables named character vector containing environment variables values passed script execution time. handled '-v' directive TORQUE clusters '–export' Slurm clusters. names vector environment variable names values vector environment variable values passed . want propagate current value environment variable compute node runtime, use NA value element env_variables. See examples. export_all Whether export environment variables compute node runtime. Default: FALSE echo Whether echo job submission command terminal time scheduled. Default: TRUE. fail_on_error Whether stop execution script (TRUE), issue warning (FALSE) job submission fails. Defaults FALSE (.e., issue warning). wait_jobs character string jobs process ids complete job executed wait_signal torque slurm clusters, signal indicate parent jobs finished. repolling_interval number seconds wait rechecking job status (used local scheduler)

title: This function submits a single script to a high-performance cluster using a scheduler (Slurm or TORQUE). It accepts a vector of arguments to be passed to the scheduler and a vector of environment variables that should be passed to the compute node at job execution. — cluster_job_submit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   #simple PBS submission   cluster_job_submit('myscript.bash', scheduler="torque", sched_args=c('-l walltime=10:00:00', '-l nodes=1:ppn=20'),      env_variables=c(RUN_INDEX=2, MODEL_NAME='FSE21'))    #To forward environment variables without explicitly providing values. Note that these must   #  be in R's system environment (cf. Sys.getenv) at execution time to forward as expected.   cluster_job_submit('myscript.sbatch', scheduler="slurm",      sched_args=c('-p general', '-N 1', '-n 12', '--mem=10g', '-t 02-00:00:00'),      env_variables=c(RUN_INDEX=2, R_HOME=NA, JAVA_HOME=NA)) } # }

title: Core function for computing multivariate time series compression scores by principal components — compress_mts_pca
text: function accepts time x signals (e.g., voxels) time series matrix. computes   singular value decomposition (SVD) examines many eigenvectors needed explain least   pexp_target proportion variance. Compression scores normalized 0 – 1.0 equation: 1 - (n_components / n_timeseries). Thus,   6 components explain 91 Given number eigenvectors integer, linear approximation exact proportion variance   explained also calculated. example, 3 components explain 84   92   get us precisely 90

text: extends fmri.stimulus allowing two normalization approaches (building AFNI dmUBLOCK):   1) "evtmax_1": pre-convolution HRF max=1.0 normalization stimulus regardless duration: identical dmUBLOCK(1)   2) "durmax_1": pre-convolution HRF max=1.0 normalization long events (15+ sec) – height HRF modulated duration        event: identical dmUBLOCK(0)

title: This function convolves a regressor with a normalized HRF whose peak amplitude is 1.0 — convolve_regressor
code: convolve_regressor(   n_vols,   reg,   tr = 1,   normalization = "none",   rm_zeros = TRUE,   center_values = TRUE,   convmax_1 = FALSE,   demean_convolved = FALSE,   high_pass = NULL,   convolve = TRUE,   ts_multiplier = NULL,   hrf_parameters = c(a1 = 6, a2 = 12, b1 = 0.9, b2 = 0.9, cc = 0.35),   microtime_resolution = 20L,   lg = NULL )

title: This function convolves a regressor with a normalized HRF whose peak amplitude is 1.0 — convolve_regressor
text: n_vols number volumes (scans) output convolved regressor reg matrix containing trial, onset, duration, value event tr repetition time seconds normalization HRF normalization method used: "none", "durmax_1", "evtmax_1" rm_zeros Whether remove zeros events vector prior convolution. Generally good idea since typically center values prior convolution, retaining zeros lead non-zero mean centering. center_values Whether demean values vector convolution. Default TRUE. convmax_1 Whether rescale convolved regressor maximum height 1. demean_convolved Whether demean regressor convolution (default: TRUE) high_pass cutoff frequency (Hz) used high-pass filtering. NULL, filtering applied. convolve TRUE, regressor convolved HRF. FALSE, regressor values simply aligned onto time grid without convolution based corresponding onsets, durations, values. ts_multiplier vector n_vols length multiplied stimulus vector convolution. hrf_parameters named vector parameters passed fmri.stimulus control shape double gamma HRF. Default: c(a1 = 6, a2 = 12, b1 = 0.9, b2 = 0.9, cc = 0.35). microtime_resolution Number microtime bins per TR used evaluating normalizing convolved regressor. lg lgr object used logging messages

title: This function convolves a regressor with a normalized HRF whose peak amplitude is 1.0 — convolve_regressor
text: Michael Hallquist

title: Output GLM configuration to a '.yaml' or 'json' file — export_glm_config
text: function exports configuration level 1, 2, 3 GLM models `glm_pipeline_arguments`   (aka 'gpa') object YAML JSON file. allows user setup models configuration   file, rather go building process manually (prompts). Typically, user first run `build_l1_models`, `build_l2_models` (multi-run data), `build_l3_models`,   run `export_glm_config` decisions made build process codified resulting file.

title: Output GLM configuration to a '.yaml' or 'json' file — export_glm_config
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   gpa <- setup_glm_pipeline(analysis_name="flanker_test",     output_directory="/proj/mnhallqlab/no_backup/flanker_gnomes",     n_expected_runs = 2, tr = 2.0,     run_data = run_df, subject_data = subject_df, trial_data = trial_df,     l1_models=NULL, l2_models=NULL, l3_models=NULL   )   # setup level 1 models through menu system  gpa <- build_l1_models(gpa)   # for multi-run data, setup level 2 models through menu system  gpa <- build_l2_models(gpa)   # setup level 3 models through menu system  gpa <- build_l3_models(gpa)   # export model configuration to YAML file  export_glm_config(gpa, file="my_glm_config.yaml")   # in future, we can build the models from the file, rather than menus  gpa <- build_l1_models(gpa, from_spec_file="my_glm_config.yaml")  gpa <- build_l2_models(gpa, from_spec_file="my_glm_config.yaml")  gpa <- build_l3_models(gpa, from_spec_file="my_glm_config.yaml") } # }

text: gpa glm_pipeline_arguments object results GLM analyses already available mask_files vector filenames NIfTI-based atlases. function loop extract coefficients unique value mask. statistics extract parcel. Default 'cope' (aka 'beta') 'zstat'. out_dir directory statistics written .csv.gz files. extract_l1 character vector l1 models extract run-level (level 1) statistics. "none", l1 statistic extraction skipped. "", l1 models extracted. extract_l2 character vector l2 models extract subject-level (level 2) statistics. "none", l2 statistic extraction skipped. "", l2 models extracted (still prompted l1 models want extract l2 model). extract_l3 character vector l3 models extract group-level (level 3) statistics. "none", l3 statistic extraction skipped. "", l3 models extracted (still prompted l1 l2 models want extract l3 model). ncores number cores use extracting statistics lower-level imgages. NULL, lookup value gpa$parallel$extract_glm_betas_ncores. value > 1 scheduler = "local", mclapply used locally extract. scheduler scheduler use extracting statistics. "local", use lapply/mclapply within current compute session. 'slurm', use doFuture multiple slurm jobs extract. aggregate whether take average (central tendency measure) voxels within given mask value. pertains integer-valued masks, continuous ones. aggFUN function used aggregate statistics voxels within given mask value. Default mean. return_data TRUE, extracted statistics returned list data.frames elements l1, l2, l3. write_data TRUE, extracted statistics written .csv.gz files out_dir.

title: Convolve a regressor with a hemodynamic response function for fMRI analysis. — fmri.stimulus
text: n_vols number volumes (scans) output convolved regressor onsets vector times (volumes seconds) specifying event onsets durations vector durations (volumes seconds) event values vector parametric values used regressor heights prior convolution units Specifies units measurement `onsets` `durations`. `"seconds"`, interpreted terms time seconds. `"volumes"`, onsets durations interpreted terms volumes scan. Thus, onset `1` volumes refers time = 0s, extension, `time = (volume - 1)*tr`. Default: `"seconds"`. center_values Whether demean values vector convolution rm_zeros Whether remove zeros events vector prior convolution. Generally good idea since typically center values prior convolution, retaining zeros lead non-zero mean centering. convolve Whether convolve regressor HRF. FALSE, time series events, durations, heights returned. tr repetition time (sometimes called TR) seconds ts_multiplier time series length n_vols multiplied stimulus convolution. demean Whether demean regressor convolution. Default: TRUE convmax_1 Whether rescale convolved regressor maximum height 1. a1 a1 parameter double gamma a2 a2 parameter double gamma b1 b1 parameter double gamma b2 b2 parameter double gamma cc cc parameter double gamma conv_method Method convolving HRF stimulus. Either "r" "cpp". "r" method uses FFT-based internal convolution convolve(x, y, conj=TRUE). "cpp" method uses internal C++ function loop-based convolution vectors. Unfortunately, present, C++ approach noticeably slower since use FFT obtain filter. microtime_resolution number bins TRs used calculating regressor values continuous time

title: This function generates syntax for FSL Feat .fsf files for the EVs tab of a first-level fMRI analysis. It accepts a numeric design matrix whose colum names correspond to individual EVs in the model — fsl_generate_fsf_lvl1_ev_syntax
text: element regressors list list specifying handle given regressor. Required elements list : Optional elements list :
code: $waveform: The type of input for this regressor. Options are:      "custom_1"    : 1-column text file containing regressor values (can be convolved or unconvolved)      "custom_3"    : 3-column FSL-format text file containing onset time, duration, and regressor value      "empty"       : an all-zero regressor      "square"      : for block design (not currently supported)      "sinusoid"    : for periodic regressors (not currently supported)      "interaction" : unclear what this is, but exists in .fsf files (not currently supported)    $convolution: The type of convolution function to be applied to the $waveform. Options are:      "none"         : no convolution is applied (esp. useful when using custom_1 files that are already convolved)      "double_gamma" : convolve with FSL-variant of double gamma function      "gaussian"     : convolve with Gaussian function (not tested)      "gamma_basis"  : Gamma basis functions (not tested)      "sine_basis"   : sine basis functions (not tested)      "fir_basis"    : finite impulse response (FIR) basis functions (not tested)      "alternate_double_gamma" : what it sounds like (not tested)    $name: The name of the EV (i.e., regressor) in the model specification and output. $timing_file: the name of the text file containing regressor values. Used by "custom_1" and "custom_3".   $tempfilt: whether to apply temporal filtering to the regressor, as with the fMRI data. Default: 1 (TRUE)   $deriv: whether to add a temporal derivative for this regressor to the model. Default 0 (FALSE)   $convolve_phase: not entirely sure what it does but is 0/1. Default: 0 (FALSE)

title: Uses an object from build_design_matrix to generate a corresponding SPM GLM design matrix. — generate_spm_mat
text: bdm object (class 'bdm') generated build_design_matrix. particular, function uses $design $run_niftis elements extract relevant regressors information NIfTI files analyze. ts_files character vector nuisance text files – one per run – added covariates SPM's multi_reg field. output_dir path SPM scripts outputs go design matrix. path can relative absolute, converted absolute internally using normalizePath(). hpf high-pass filter cutoff (seconds) used remove low frequencies prior analysis. Default: 100 hrf_derivs Whether include derivatives regressors model capture HRF variation. Options : "none", "time" (temporal derivative), "time_dispersion" (temporal dispersion derivatives). Default: "none" cvi Serial correlations model. Options : "AR(1)", "FAST", "none". Default: "AR(1)" estimation_method Estimation method. Options : "Classical" "Bayesian2". Default: "Classical" write_residuals Whether write residual images. supported Classical estimation. Default: FALSE fmri_t Microtime resolution (number time bins per scan). Default: 20 fmri_t0 Microtime onset (reference bin). Default: 1 nifti_tmpdir place uncompressed NIfTIs analysis (SPM handle .nii.gz) cleanup_tmp Whether remove uncompressed .nii files completing function condition_contrasts Whether setup contrasts condition. multi-run data, contrasts represent condition average across runs (e.g., [ .5, .5 ] 2 runs). Default: TRUE unit_contrasts Whether estimate unit-height contrast every regressor design. essentially includes diagonal matrix contrast specification. Default: FALSE effects_of_interest_F Whether include single F test contrast regressors interest. Useful adjusting VOIs nuisance regressors. Default: TRUE spm_execute_setup Whether run GLM setup script MATLAB, creating SPM.mat. Default: FALSE spm_execute_glm Whether run GLM creating SPM.mat. take ! Default: FALSE spm_execute_contrasts Whether compute contrasts GLM complete. Depends spm_execute_glm. Default: FALSE concatenate_runs Whether convert multi-run data single concatenated session. execute spm_fmri_concatenate GLM setup complete. script computes run-specific whitening high-pass filters keeping single concatenated time series. useful preamble DCM, often uses concatenated time series. Default: FALSE spm_path path spm12 directory. included MATLAB scripts ensure spm found. matlab_cmd Command launch MATLAB/Octave. Default: "matlab" matlab_args Arguments passed MATLAB/Octave. Default: "-batch" compute_env Optional character vector shell commands prepare environment (e.g., module load statements). prepended direct MATLAB execution. include_block_contrasts Whether estimate contrasts run-specific intercepts design. Note qualifies condition_contrasts unit_contrasts, nothing .

text: function intended setup first-level (subject) fMRI GLM analysis SPM12. accepts object build_design_matrix uses contents object setup timing HRF settings (including parametric modulation) regressors. point, function flexible terms allowing custom tweaks field. mostly intended setup necessary SPM structures support DCM, depends SPM.mat file. discovered spm12r package recently, lot flexibility setting first-level model. future, may wish adapt code use instead. https://cran.rstudio.com/web/packages/spm12r/vignettes/fmri_task_processing.html

title: Function to output l1_models into '.yaml' object — get_l1_config
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   gpa <- setup_glm_pipeline(analysis_name="flanker_test",     output_directory="/proj/mnhallqlab/no_backup/flanker_gnomes",     n_expected_runs = 2, tr = 2.0,     run_data = run_df, subject_data = subject_df, trial_data = trial_df,     l1_models=NULL, l2_models=NULL, l3_models=NULL   )   # setup level 1 models through menu system  gpa <- build_l1_models(gpa)   # get level 1 model settings as a list  l1_settings <- get_l1_config(gpa)   # we should typically use export_glm_config to write to file, but we could convert manually  l1_yaml <- as.yaml(l1_settings)  writeLines(l1_yaml, "my_l1_config.yaml")   # in future, we can populate the models using  gpa <- build_l1_models(gpa, from_spec_file="my_l1_config.yaml") } # }

title: front-end function for taking a list of windowed time series by mask value, interpolating them onto a time grid, and (optionally) averaging across voxels/units within a value to derive the mean interpolated time series — get_medusa_compression_score
text: fmri_obj fmri_ts object containing single run data corresponding events event event fmri time series aligned (column fmri_obj$event_data) time_before many seconds event want data. Default: -3 time_after many seconds event want data. Default: 3 collide_before optional vector column names trial_df set boundary earliest time point used interpolation. effectively truncates time series interpolation smaller window specified time_before. collide_after optional vector column names trial_df set boundary latest time point used interpolation. effectively truncates time series interpolation smaller window specified time_after. group_by return interpolated time series combination group_by variables. Default provide one interpolated time series per trial. pad_before Number seconds include epoch time window event interest. Interpolation spans window time_before time_after, padding includes data points boundary can help sufficient data interpolate early late times within epoch. pad_after Number seconds include epoch time window event interest. output_resolution sampling frequency (seconds) interpolated data. Defaults tr.
